WI.hero_title: Wisconsin: Where ThedaCare Leads and Froedtert Lags
WI.hero_subtitle: The state with the highest median transparency â€” and the sharpest system-level contrasts.
WI.intro_text: ThedaCare achieves 100% transparency at all 9 facilities. Froedtert Hospital â€” Milwaukee's flagship â€” discloses rates for only 6 payers. UW Health discloses 112 payer contracts but remains on the outdated v2.0.0 schema.
findings: ThedaCare is the transparency gold standard: 100% payer rate disclosure at all 9 facilities, up to 59 payers, current v3.0.0 schema, updated March 2026.
Froedtert Hospital â€” Milwaukee's largest, Level I Trauma Center â€” discloses rates for only 6 payers. A 25-bed critical access hospital in rural Boscobel (Gundersen) discloses more.

UW Health discloses the most payer contracts in the state (112) using percentage-of-gross methodology, but its file is stale (January 2025) and still on v2.0.0 â€” at risk for CMS enforcement beginning April 2026.
Ascension's three Wisconsin facilities are the least transparent (27â€“38%), consistent with national reporting on Ascension's disclosure approach.
Marshfield's 5% cash discount is effectively no discount â€” a patient saves $50 on a $1,000 procedure. The lowest meaningful discount in the state.
Gundersen's intra-system cash discount variance spans 21% to 62% across seven facilities â€” evidence of facility-level pricing autonomy.

MN.hero_subtitle: A consumer-governed nonprofit that hides what insurance actually pays.
MN.intro_text: HealthPartners â€” both an insurer and a hospital system â€” scores 6.9% at Regions Hospital. They give patients generous cash discounts (53â€“75% off gross) while hiding what insured patients' carriers actually negotiate.
findings: HealthPartners Regions Hospital â€” Level I Trauma, 454 beds, consumer-governed nonprofit â€” discloses payer rates on 6.9% of rows. The lowest non-zero score in the three-state dataset.
All four HealthPartners facilities score below 12% â€” the only system in either state entirely below 20%. Yet their cash discounts are the highest in Minnesota (53â€“75%).
Mayo Clinic Rochester discloses rates for 1,102 payer/plan combinations â€” 10x more than any other hospital in any state. The richest single chargemaster file in the dataset.
Allina Health achieves 99.8â€“100% across all 5 analyzed facilities â€” proving full disclosure is operationally feasible for a large multi-hospital system.
Essentia Brainerd (St. Joseph's Medical Center) scores 99.6% with 39 payers â€” one of the most transparent hospitals in the dataset and the strongest local data anchor.
M Health Fairview shows good percentage transparency (70â€“90%) but only 2â€“4 payers per facility â€” the structure has rates but for very few contracts.

IL.hero_subtitle: Northwestern and Rush comply fully. UChicago publishes almost nothing.
IL.intro_text: Three of Chicago's four academic flagships comply comprehensively. UChicago Medical Center discloses payer rates for only 2.9% of items â€” exclusively DRG bundles â€” while individual line items show only list prices.
findings: UChicago Medical Center discloses payer rates for 2.9% of items (2,302 of 78,504). Payer data exists only on DRG bundles; 97% of items show only gross charges and cash prices. Strategic selective disclosure.
Northwestern Memorial Hospital achieves ~95% transparency with 32 payers across v3.0.0 JSON â€” comprehensive disclosure from Chicago's largest hospital.
Rush University Medical Center hits 100% with 35 payers â€” proving Chicago's academic centers can comply fully.
Advocate Health is remarkably uniform: all 11 facilities cluster at 60â€“70% with exactly 18 payers and a 50% cash discount. Centralized but incomplete.
HSHS's Illinois hospitals (100%, 30â€“34 payers) outperform Ascension's Illinois presence (32â€“67%) â€” smaller system, better disclosure.
Loyola Gottlieb offers an 81% cash discount â€” the highest in the three-state dataset â€” while disclosing only 56% of payer rates.

IA.hero_title: Iowa: MercyOne’s 755 Payers Set the Midwest Standard
IA.hero_subtitle: 94.4% median — MercyOne and UnityPoint cover the state with very different approaches.
IA.intro_text: Iowa’s 94.4% median transparency ranks among the highest in the Midwest once non-Iowa entries are cleaned from the data. MercyOne achieves 94–99% across 13 facilities with an extraordinary 755+ payer contracts each. UnityPoint clusters at 51–75% with a uniform 20% cash discount.
findings: MercyOne is Iowa's transparency leader: 13 facilities at 94–99.5% transparency, each disclosing rates for 752–760 payers. No other Midwest system comes close to this payer breadth.
UnityPoint Health (14 facilities) clusters in the Substantial tier at 51–75% — all on v3.0.0 schema with a uniform 20% cash discount. Centralized pricing, incomplete disclosure.
University of Iowa Health Care hits 100% across both campuses — the state's flagship academic center complies fully.
Iowa Specialty Hospital (Clarion + Belmond) both achieve 100% with 12–15 payers and a 40% cash discount — proof that small hospitals can fully comply.
MercyOne's cash discount variance spans 9–36% across facilities — compared to UnityPoint's uniform 20%. MercyOne appears to set pricing at the facility level; UnityPoint centralizes it.
14 hospitals scored 0% — mostly non-Iowa facilities from national cms-hpt.txt files (Gundersen, Trinity, Genesis) that were incorrectly attributed to Iowa in discovery.

IN.hero_title: Indiana: IU Health’s Perfect Score Transforms the State
IN.hero_subtitle: 16 IU Health facilities at 100%. Parkview’s 10 facilities at 1–4%. Two Indianas.
IN.intro_text: IU Health — Indiana’s largest system — achieves 100% transparency at all 16 facilities with 20–29 payers and cash discounts of 53–79%. Parkview Health in Fort Wayne scores 0.5–4.4% across 10 facilities — files are JSON disguised as CSV, and what parses contains almost no negotiated rates.
findings: IU Health achieves 100% transparency at all 16 facilities — 20–29 payers, 53–79% cash discounts. Indiana’s gold standard and one of the best system-wide performances in the Midwest.
Parkview Health (10 Fort Wayne-area facilities) scores 0.5–4.4% — files are JSON served as CSV, and what parses contains almost no negotiated rates. Minimal compliance.
Deaconess (Evansville) clusters at 54–74% (Substantial) with 7–20 payers and generous cash discounts of 41–70%.
Riley Children’s Hospital at IU Health hits 100% with 29 payers — full compliance at Indiana’s flagship pediatric center.
IU Health’s cash discounts (53–79%) are among the highest in the Midwest — a self-pay patient at IU Health Bedford saves 70% off gross charges.
Community Health Network — despite 17 hospitals in CMS inventory — only Stones Crossing Imaging Center was discoverable (100%, 51 payers).

KS.hero_title: Kansas: KU Health Leads, AdventHealth Pollutes the Data
KS.hero_subtitle: 7 Full-tier hospitals buried under 40 misattributed national entries.
KS.intro_text: Kansas has a data quality problem. AdventHealth’s national cms-hpt.txt dumps 38 non-Kansas facilities into our discovery pipeline — all scoring 0%. Strip those out and Kansas looks better: KU Health System achieves 100% across 5 facilities, and Stormont Vail in Topeka hits 80–86%.
findings: University of Kansas Health System achieves 100% transparency at all 5 facilities with 12–70 payers and cash discounts of 76–80%.
Stormont Vail (Topeka) hits 80–86% at 2 facilities with 8 payers — solid regional performer.
Hays Medical Center achieves 100% with 194 payers — one of the highest payer counts in the Midwest.
38 AdventHealth entries score 0% — these are Florida, Texas, and Colorado facilities pulled from AdventHealth’s national txt file, not Kansas hospitals.
Only 2 actual Kansas-specific health systems discovered (KU, Stormont Vail) — the rest of the state’s 136 hospitals lack discoverable chargemasters.
Mercy Hospital Pittsburg discloses only 1.4% of payer rates despite listing 22 payers — minimal compliance.

MI.hero_title: Michigan: McLaren’s Perfect Score, Corewell’s Black Box
MI.hero_subtitle: 12 McLaren hospitals at 100%. 19 Corewell hospitals at 0%. Same state, opposite choices.
MI.intro_text: Michigan is the Midwest’s starkest transparency divide. McLaren Health Care achieves 100% at all 12 facilities with 22–38 payers and a uniform 50% cash discount. Corewell Health — Michigan’s largest system (formerly Beaumont/Spectrum) — registers 0% across all 19 facilities despite listing 14–21 payers in their files.
findings: McLaren Health Care achieves 100% transparency at all 12 facilities — 22–38 payers, uniform 50% cash discount. The gold standard for Michigan.
Corewell Health (19 facilities, Michigan’s largest) scores 0% everywhere despite payer names appearing in files. Structural non-disclosure from the state’s dominant system.
Henry Ford spans the full range: Providence Novi/Southfield at 91% (Full), flagship Detroit at 44% (Partial), Kingswood behavioral at 0.1%. Wide intra-system variance.
Karmanos Cancer Institute hits 100% with 32 payers — full compliance from a specialty cancer center.
Munson Healthcare (8 facilities in northern MI) clusters at 45–85% with only 2–3 payers each — good rates but very few payers disclosed.
Corewell’s 0% is the biggest finding — a 19-hospital system serving metro Detroit and Grand Rapids that technically publishes files but discloses no actual negotiated rates.

MO.hero_title: Missouri: BJC Shines, Mercy Hides, SSM Blocks
MO.hero_subtitle: Three systems, three disclosure philosophies — and a polarized landscape.
MO.intro_text: Missouri’s 15.6% median masks a deeply polarized landscape. BJC Healthcare and CoxHealth achieve 100% at their flagship facilities. Mercy — Missouri’s largest Catholic system — shows 99% at small Stoddard County clinics but 1–16% at its major hospitals. SSM Health returns 0% across all entries.
findings: BJC Healthcare (Barnes-Jewish, St. Louis Children’s) achieves 92–100% across 13 facilities — comprehensive disclosure from Missouri’s premier academic system.
CoxHealth hits 100% at all 4 Springfield-area facilities with 254–304 payers — extraordinary payer breadth.
Mercy’s paradox: small rural clinics (Stoddard, Sikeston) disclose 98–99%, but major hospitals score Minimal (1–16%) or failed to download. Size correlates inversely with transparency.
SSM Health returns 0% at all 21 entries — consistent with TLS/format blocking seen in Wisconsin and Illinois.
Mercy rehab hospitals cluster at 5–14% with 0% cash discount — the lowest cash pricing transparency in the state.
34 Full-tier hospitals is misleading — many are small Mercy/CHI clinics. The major trauma centers are where the gaps are.

ND.hero_title: North Dakota: Sanford’s Solid Middle, CHI’s Full Marks
ND.hero_subtitle: No hospitals below 20% — the Midwest’s most consistent disclosure state.
ND.intro_text: North Dakota is a transparency success story. Sanford Health — the state’s dominant system — clusters at 57–83% (Substantial tier) across its network. CHI Health facilities achieve 100%. Only 3 of 40 profiled hospitals scored 0%, and those appear to be non-ND entries from national txt files.
findings: Sanford Health is consistently Substantial: 57–83% transparency, 4–17 payers, uniform 20% cash discount. Not Full tier, but reliably above 50%.
CHI Health facilities achieve 100% — CommonSpirit’s strongest state performance in the Midwest.
No Minimal-tier hospitals — North Dakota is one of only 3 Midwest states (with NE, SD) where every real hospital scores above 20%.
40 of 46 hospitals profiled — 96% discovery rate, the highest in the Midwest.
Sanford Bemidji and Sanford USD Medical Center score lower (57–74%) with only 4 payers — larger facilities disclose fewer payer contracts.
North Dakota’s success reflects Sanford Health’s standardized compliance approach across facilities.

NE.hero_title: Nebraska: The Midwest’s Quiet Transparency Leader
NE.hero_subtitle: 79.1% median — the highest in the Midwest, with zero hospitals at 0%.
NE.intro_text: Nebraska has the highest median transparency in the Midwest at 79.1%, and every profiled hospital discloses at least some payer rates. CHI Health anchors the state with consistent performance, and Nebraska Medicine (UNMC) contributes strong academic disclosure.
findings: Every profiled hospital discloses payer rates — Nebraska is the only Midwest state with zero None-tier hospitals.
CHI Health achieves consistent Full/Substantial performance across its Nebraska network.
Nebraska Medicine (University of Nebraska Medical Center) provides strong academic hospital disclosure.
79.1% median exceeds every other Midwest state including North Dakota (76.7%) and South Dakota (76.8%).
Only 19 of 92 non-VA hospitals profiled — good scores but limited coverage.
Mercy presence in Nebraska scores Minimal — consistent with Mercy’s pattern across MO and KS.

OH.hero_title: Ohio: Cleveland Clinic Discloses, OhioHealth Stays Dark
OH.hero_subtitle: The Clinic averages 53% across 19 hospitals. OhioHealth’s 16 facilities remain at 0%.
OH.intro_text: Ohio’s transparency picture improved dramatically once Cleveland Clinic’s ZIP-compressed files were properly parsed. The Clinic averages 53% (Substantial) across 19 hospitals with 19–46 payers. TriHealth in Cincinnati hits 76–79%. But OhioHealth — Columbus’s dominant system — remains at 0%, and Kettering Health in Dayton scores 0.4–2.1%.
findings: Cleveland Clinic averages 53% transparency across 19 Ohio hospitals with 19–46 payers and a uniform 35% cash discount. The flagship Main Campus scores 38.5% — lower than its community hospitals.
TriHealth (Cincinnati) is Ohio’s transparency leader: 76–79% at all 6 facilities with 114 payers and 25% cash discount.
OhioHealth (16 Columbus-area hospitals) remains at 0% — files are valid JSON but the streaming profiler finds no negotiated dollar values. A structural disclosure gap.
Kettering Health (9 Dayton-area hospitals) scores 0.4–2.1% — technically not zero, but functionally useless for price comparison.
ProMedica and Aultman anchor northwest and northeast Ohio with consistent Full-tier performance.
Cleveland Clinic Main Campus at 38.5% is a notable finding — the nation’s #2-ranked hospital discloses payer rates for barely a third of its services.

SD.hero_subtitle: Two systems cover the state. Both disclose. No gaps.
SD.intro_text: South Dakota’s hospital market is a duopoly: Sanford Health and Avera Health between them cover nearly every bed in the state. Both disclose consistently, producing a 76.8% median with zero None-tier hospitals.
findings: Avera Health and Sanford Health together account for nearly all 27 profiled hospitals — a true duopoly market.
Zero None-tier hospitals — South Dakota joins Nebraska as a state where every profiled hospital discloses some payer rates.
Monument Health (Rapid City) contributes additional coverage in western South Dakota.
76.8% median ranks third in the Midwest behind Nebraska (79.1%) and North Dakota (76.7%).
The duopoly structure makes South Dakota unusually transparent — two standardized systems cover the entire state.
27 of 59 non-VA hospitals profiled — 46% discovery rate. Missing hospitals are likely small critical access facilities.
